title: The Bare-Metal Mindset & the Memory Map

lessons.title: Memory Is Silicon, Not an Array
lessons.content:
On a hosted machine, memory feels like one flat, uniform array of bytes that a runtime hands you. On a microcontroller (MCU), that abstraction is gone. The CPU drives a single linear **address space** (4 GB on a 32-bit Cortex-M), but different *ranges* of that space are wired to physically different things.

Three regions dominate:

- **Flash** — non-volatile, read/execute. Your code (`.text`), constants (`.rodata`), and the reset vectors live here. It survives power-off. You cannot casually store to it; writing flash means erase-then-program a whole page.
- **SRAM** — volatile read/write. Your variables (`.data`, `.bss`), the stack, and any heap live here. Fast, but small (often kilobytes) and gone at reset.
- **Peripheral region** — addresses that are not memory at all. They are *control and status registers* hard-wired into devices (GPIO, timers, UART).

The load-bearing reframe for a software dev: an address is not a slot that just holds a value. The *same* `STR` (store) instruction that writes a struct field can, at a different address, physically toggle a pin or launch a DMA transfer. Where the address points decides what the bus does.

```c
/* All three are just stores -- the address decides the effect. */
uint32_t x;
x = 1;                       /* writes SRAM, no side effect */
*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020018 = 1; /* writes a GPIO register: a pin goes high */
```

You studied this exact bus in your digital course; here it is the silicon under your C.

items.front: Name the three dominant regions of a Cortex-M address space and what each is for.
items.back: Flash: non-volatile read/execute -- code, constants, vectors. SRAM: volatile read/write -- variables, stack, heap. Peripheral region: addresses that ARE device registers (GPIO, timers, UART), not storage.
items.hint: One survives power-off, one is fast scratch, one isn't memory at all.

items.front: Why is 'memory is a flat array of objects' a misleading model on an MCU?
items.back: Because the address space is heterogeneous: ranges map to physically different hardware -- flash, SRAM, or peripheral registers. The effect of a store depends on WHERE it targets, not just the value written.
items.hint: Think about what a store does at a peripheral address.

items.front: On an MCU, where do global/static variables (.data and .bss) physically live at run time?
items.back: In SRAM. (.data is initialized from a copy stored in flash; .bss is zeroed. Both reside in SRAM once running.)
items.hint: Volatile, fast, read/write, lost at reset.

items.front: Why can't you treat flash like ordinary RAM and just store individual bytes to it?
items.back: Flash is non-volatile and must be erased a whole page/sector at a time before re-programming; it has limited write endurance. It is read/execute in normal operation, not arbitrarily byte-writable.

items.prompt: A 32-bit Cortex-M executes `*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020018 = 1;`. The pin wired to that bit goes high. What does this demonstrate?
choices: The compiler special-cased the address into a GPIO intrinsic
A plain store instruction to a peripheral address has a physical side effect because the address is a hardware register
Writing to flash toggled the pin
The value 1 is interpreted as a voltage by the CPU core
items.explanation: The store is an ordinary STR; the address falls in the peripheral region where that location is a GPIO output register, so the bus write physically drives the pin.

items.prompt: After a power cycle, which contents are guaranteed to be lost?
choices: The contents of flash (.text and .rodata)
The reset vector
The contents of SRAM (variables, stack, heap)
Nothing; an MCU retains all memory across power cycles
items.explanation: SRAM is volatile and loses its contents at power-off; flash is non-volatile and persists, which is why code and constants live there.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the 'bare-metal mindset' shift from application programming?
choices: Pointers no longer hold addresses
Memory is a managed abstraction provided by a runtime
Memory is a physical address space where the target address determines whether you touch RAM, flash, or a live device register
All memory accesses are equally fast and side-effect free
items.explanation: The core reframe is that the address space is physical and heterogeneous; the address decides what the access actually does.

items.prompt: Why does code typically execute directly from flash on a small MCU rather than being copied to RAM first?
choices: Flash is faster than SRAM for execution
SRAM is usually tiny and non-persistent, while flash is non-volatile, larger, and execute-in-place capable
The CPU cannot fetch instructions from SRAM
Flash cannot store data, only instructions
items.explanation: MCUs have far more flash than SRAM and flash retains code across resets, so execute-in-place from flash is the norm; SRAM is reserved for the small, mutable working set.

lessons.title: Memory-Mapped I/O, Word Size, Alignment & Endianness
lessons.content:
In the peripheral region, **memory-mapped I/O (MMIO)** means a load or store *is* the device transaction. This breaks two assumptions a desktop C programmer leans on.

**Writes have side effects.** Storing to a GPIO set/reset register drives pins; storing to a UART data register launches a byte on the wire. The value you wrote may not even be readable back.

**Reads have side effects too.** Reading a status register can clear a flag. A classic UART pattern: reading the data register clears the RXNE 'data ready' bit. So a stray, optimizer-inserted, or duplicated read can *eat* an event. This is exactly why register accesses must be `volatile` -- the compiler must perform every access you wrote, no more and no fewer, and must not cache or reorder them.

**Word size & alignment.** Cortex-M is a 32-bit machine: the natural word is 4 bytes. Registers are accessed at their natural width and natural alignment. A 32-bit access to an address not divisible by 4 can raise a **UsageFault** (unaligned access), and many peripherals *only* accept word-width access. Packed protocol structs that misalign fields are a frequent fault source.

```c
/* uint32_t access must be 4-byte aligned */
volatile uint32_t *reg = (volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014;
uint32_t status = *reg;   /* one real bus read -- may clear a flag */
```

**Endianness.** Most Cortex-M parts are **little-endian**: the least-significant byte sits at the lowest address. It is invisible within the chip but bites the moment you serialize multi-byte values for a protocol or parse a big-endian network/sensor frame. Always byte-pack explicitly rather than casting a struct over a buffer.

items.front: In memory-mapped I/O, what surprising thing can a simple READ of a register do?
items.back: It can have a side effect -- e.g. reading a data register clears the 'data ready' / RXNE flag. A spurious or duplicated read can therefore lose an event, which is why such accesses must be volatile.

items.front: What does `volatile` on a register pointer force the compiler to do?
items.back: Perform exactly the accesses written in source -- no caching the value in a CPU register, no eliding 'redundant' reads/writes, and no reordering volatile accesses relative to each other.
items.hint: Every access in source becomes one real bus access.

items.front: What is the natural word size of a Cortex-M, and what happens on a misaligned 32-bit access?
items.back: 32 bits (4 bytes). A 32-bit access to an address not divisible by 4 can raise a UsageFault (unaligned access); many peripherals also require word-width access only.

items.front: Most Cortex-M cores are little-endian. Where does endianness actually bite you?
items.back: In serialization/protocol code: when packing multi-byte values onto a wire or parsing a big-endian frame. Inside the chip it's invisible; across a link it corrupts data if you cast a struct over a byte buffer instead of packing explicitly.

items.prompt: A UART driver reads the data register twice 'to be safe.' Bytes go missing. Why?
choices: The second read corrupts the alignment of the buffer
Reading the data register clears RXNE, so the second read consumes/discards a fresh byte's state
Two reads cause an unaligned access fault
The compiler merged both reads into one
items.explanation: Reads of MMIO registers can have side effects; reading the data register clears the data-ready flag, so an extra read mis-handles the receive state and effectively eats data.

items.prompt: Which is a guarantee `volatile` does NOT provide?
choices: Each source-level access becomes a real memory access
Volatile accesses are not cached in CPU registers
Atomicity and a full CPU memory barrier across all accesses
Volatile accesses are not reordered relative to each other
items.explanation: volatile controls that accesses happen and are not elided or reordered among themselves, but it does not provide atomicity or ordering against non-volatile accesses; for that you need atomics or barriers (DMB/DSB/ISB).

items.prompt: On a little-endian Cortex-M, the 32-bit value 0x11223344 is stored at address A. Which byte is at address A (the lowest)?
items.explanation: Little-endian places the least-significant byte at the lowest address, so 0x44 is at A and 0x11 at A+3.

items.prompt: A peripheral register block is overlaid with a packed struct, and one 32-bit field lands at an offset of 2 bytes. A word read of it faults. The root cause is:
choices: The register is read-only
Endianness mismatch between host and target
A 32-bit access to a non-4-byte-aligned address violated natural alignment
volatile was missing from the struct
items.explanation: A 32-bit access must be 4-byte aligned on Cortex-M; an offset of 2 is misaligned and can raise a UsageFault. Packed structs that misalign word fields are a common cause.

lessons.title: Stack vs Heap With No OS, and the Path to main()

items.front: On a typical MCU, which direction do the stack and heap grow within SRAM, and what guards the gap between them?
items.back: The stack grows downward from the top of SRAM; the heap grows upward from above .bss. Nothing inherently guards the gap -- with no MMU, a stack overflow silently corrupts whatever is below it.
items.hint: They grow toward each other; collision = silent corruption.

items.front: Why is a stack overflow on bare metal especially dangerous compared to a hosted OS?
items.back: There is no MMU/guard page to trap it, so it doesn't fault cleanly -- it silently overwrites adjacent SRAM (variables or heap), and the symptom appears far from the cause.

items.front: List the main reasons dynamic allocation (malloc/free) is discouraged in bare-metal firmware.
items.back: Non-deterministic timing, heap fragmentation in tiny RAM leading to unpredictable allocation failure, usually not ISR-safe, and no sane recovery when it returns NULL in the field. Prefer static allocation: fixed pools, ring buffers, sized buffers.

items.front: What does the reset/startup code do before it ever calls main()?
items.back: It sets the initial stack pointer (from the first vector-table word), copies .data from its flash load image into SRAM, zeroes .bss, optionally inits the heap and runs C-lib/constructor init, then calls main. main must never return.
items.hint: Copy .data, zero .bss, set SP, then main.

items.prompt: What does 'there is no main() until you write the path to it' mean on a Cortex-M?
choices: main() is forbidden in embedded C
The reset handler and linker script -- not a hidden runtime -- are what initialize memory and reach main(); main is just a function the startup code calls
main() must be written in assembly
The CPU jumps directly to main() at reset with no setup
items.explanation: At reset the CPU runs the reset handler from the vector table; that startup code initializes .data/.bss and then calls main. There is no implicit runtime -- the startup code and linker script are the runtime you own.

items.prompt: At reset on a Cortex-M, the very first two words of the vector table provide:
choices: The heap base and heap limit
The initial stack pointer and the reset handler address
The clock frequency and flash wait states
The addresses of main() and exit()
items.explanation: Word 0 is the initial MSP value loaded into the stack pointer; word 1 is the reset handler address the CPU jumps to. Other vectors follow.

items.prompt: Why must .data be copied from flash to SRAM at startup, while .bss is merely zeroed?
choices: .data holds initialized variables whose initial values are stored in flash but must live in writable SRAM; .bss is zero-initialized so only needs clearing
.bss is read-only and stays in flash
.data executes and .bss does not
There is no difference; both are copied identically
items.explanation: .data variables have nonzero initial values kept as a load image in flash and must be copied into their writable SRAM home; .bss variables start at zero, so the startup code just zeroes that SRAM range.

items.prompt: A firmware engineer wants deterministic, fragmentation-free buffers. Which approach best fits the bare-metal discipline?
choices: Call malloc() in each ISR and free() in main
Use a global recursive allocator with garbage collection
Statically allocate fixed-size pools and ring buffers whose footprint is visible in the map file
Grow the stack dynamically as needed
items.explanation: Static allocation -- fixed pools, ring buffers, sized buffers -- gives deterministic timing and a known, map-file-visible memory footprint, avoiding heap fragmentation and ISR-unsafe allocation.
